title: How MVCC and Transactions Work in RocksDB
excerpt: RocksDB is built on an LSM-tree, which never modifies data in-place - every write creates a new version of the key. That's half of what you need to implement Multi-Version Concurrency Control (MVCC).

RocksDB is built on an LSM-tree, which never modifies data in-place - every write creates a new version of the key. That's half of what you need to implement Multi-Version Concurrency Control (MVCC).

Real-world databases serve many clients reading and writing the same data at the same time. The simplest way to make concurrent access safe is locking (think of a hash map protected with a mutex). This works from the correctness perspective, but makes readers and writers block each other, which can quickly become a performance bottleneck. MVCC solves this problem, allowing readers and writers to proceed concurrently. The idea is that:

  1. Writers never modify or delete keys in-place, instead they create new versions of the keys

  2. Readers see a consistent view of the data as it existed when the read started - they access older versions of the keys, while writers keep creating new versions independently

MVCC is used in many DBs including PostgreSQL, CockroachDB, FoundationDB, MongoDB WiredTiger, and LMDB. Today we'll see how RocksDB implements the other half of MVCC - snapshots that give readers that consistent view, atomic updates and transactions built on top.

Versioning Keys #

All inserts, updates and deletes go through a write buffer (memtable) and then get flushed to disk into an immutable SST (Static Sorted Table) file - you naturally have multiple versions of the same key. Compaction is the garbage collection process that merges SSTs and removes older versions of keys.

Let's pretend our database is an insert-only list ordered by key. If we add these key-value pairs dog,a, chipmunk,a, cat,a, raccoon,a, we'll end up with a list that looks like this:

It's a toy database, so point lookup queries run by scanning the entire list. Now a query starts, trying to look up dog. While the query is running, a writer proceeds more quickly and overwrites dog with b before the reader reaches it. The reader is still at chipmunk, but the writer already finished inserting dog,b:

Should the reader see dog,b since it's the latest value inserted, or see dog,a because that was the value when the reader started the query? "Readers see a consistent view" clearly answers this - the reader should see dog,a.

Earlier I said the LSM-tree already does half of what's needed to implement MVCC, half because having immutable memtables and SSTs alone is not enough. You also need a way to provide readers a snapshot of the database, and the GC process needs some way of knowing which tables are being accessed by in-flight queries.

Internally, RocksDB assigns each inserted key a monotonically increasing number, which it calls a sequence number. When you insert a key into RocksDB, what actually gets into the memtable and SST files on disk is a triplet of (user_key, sequence_number, value_type), stored together with the value. The user key is the key you provided, the sequence number is a counter incremented on every write, and finally the value type is whether it's an insert or a delete (or other operations, which are irrelevant for this blog post).

With these, the updated representation of the list from the example before is:

RocksDB tracks two sequence numbers - the last allocated sequence (the last number assigned on write), and the published sequence (the number visible to readers, advanced after a write completes).

In our example, the last allocated sequence number is 5 - the number that was assigned to dog,b. The published sequence when the reader R started was 4, and it will be 5 after the write completes.

When a read query starts, RocksDB captures the published sequence number. This sequence is then used for querying the memtables and the SSTs on disk. The reader R, when it sees dog,b with the sequence number 5, will skip over it, as it is allowed to see only keys with a sequence number of 4 or below.

The memtable implementation in RocksDB is not that far from our toy example. As in the example we used, it's based on a linked list, specifically on a skip list. The data structure adds layers of links that allow fast search and insertion in sorted order:

Layer 0 contains every entry - like the linked list from the example before. Each next layer contains a subset of the previous layer's entries. The search starts at the sparsest layer, descending to the full linked list. This structure gives O(log n) average time complexity for search.

When comparing internal keys, RocksDB first orders by the user-provided key ascending and then by the sequence number descending, so that when two keys share the same user key, the one with the larger sequence number comes first. A read query that captured the published sequence number 4 translates into a skip list seek to dog 4. The seek starts with cat 3 at layer 1, skips to dog 5 on layer 1, descends to dog 5 on layer 0 and finally lands on dog 1. The search can stop there since in the internal key order dog 4 is larger than dog 5, but smaller than dog 1. In Rust this could be expressed with the Ord trait this way:

impl Ord for InternalKey {
    fn cmp(&self, other: &Self) -> Ordering {
        if self.user_key == other.user_key {
            // Reversed - the larger sequence number comes first.
            other.sequence_number.cmp(&self.sequence_number)
        } else {
            self.user_key.cmp(&other.user_key)
        }
    }
}

The skip list in RocksDB is concurrent, meaning it doesn't require locking, so reads and writes can truly happen concurrently. The implementation uses atomic operations on pointers. Garbage collection in concurrent algorithms in non-GC languages is a hard problem, which RocksDB solves elegantly by relying on an arena allocator - individual entries are never freed, deletes are inserts with a DELETE value type and the memory for the entire memtable is freed after the memtable is no longer in use.

Atomic Updates #

The write batch API provides atomicity - keys written in the same batch become visible together:

let mut wb = WriteBatch::new();
wb.put("a", "1");
wb.put("b", "2");
wb.put("c", "3");
db.write(wb)?;

Atomicity becomes straightforward to implement with the key versioning and sequencing mechanism we discussed above. When the batch is applied to the database (the db.write call), sequence numbers for all keys are allocated atomically by incrementing the sequence number by 3 (the number of keys in the batch) and then publishing that number only after all three keys are inserted into the memtable. This ensures no reader can see a partial batch - they either see all three keys, or none of them.

Snapshots #

Every get implicitly captures the latest published sequence number. The snapshot API pins it explicitly, so multiple reads can see the database at the same point in time:

db.put("a", "1")?;
let snapshot = db.snapshot();
db.put("a", "2")?;
snapshot.get("a")?; // Returns "1" - the value at the time the snapshot was taken.
db.get("a")?; // Returns "2" - the latest value.

A snapshot is just the published sequence number recorded when db.snapshot() is called - reads through the snapshot skip keys with higher sequence numbers. RocksDB also registers the snapshot in a list of active snapshots. Compaction checks the list and drops an older version of a key only if no live snapshot can still see it.

Versioning Tables #

Going back to GC, older key versions are removed by compactions, but how does the database know when an SST file or a memtable is safe to delete? What if there is an in-flight reader still accessing an already flushed memtable, or reading an SST that compaction is supposed to remove?

RocksDB maintains a structure called SuperVersion, which is a reference-counted view of everything needed for reads:

struct SuperVersion {
    active_memtable: ReferenceCounter<Memtable>,          // memtable accepting writes
    immutable_memtables: Vec<ReferenceCounter<Memtable>>, // memtables ready to be flushed or just flushed
    ssts: ReferenceCounter<SstLevels>,                    // on-disk SSTs, each reference-counted individually
}

When a read starts, it acquires a reference to the structure. The reference is released only after the read is done. When the number of references to SuperVersion drops to zero, it releases its child fields, decrementing their reference counts. This way neither the structure itself nor any of its children can be freed mid-read. The cleanup - freeing the memtable memory or scheduling files for deletion is performed only when a child's own reference count reaches zero.

The Wikipedia definition of MVCC says it's a "non-locking concurrency control method". In the database literature there is a distinction between locks and latches. Locks are logical - over keys, key ranges, or rows. When a lock is held on a key, nobody else can modify it until the lock is released. Latches are physical, e.g. mutexes that protect the database structures from data races during concurrent access.

By this definition, the core of RocksDB doesn't take logical key locks, and the read and write paths are designed to not hold latches in the common hot cases. Some less frequently exercised code paths, however, do require latches - e.g. there is a coarse-grained per-DB mutex held briefly after a flush or compaction to replace the current SuperVersion.

Transactions #

Let's see how we can implement a classic balance transfer example with RocksDB, where Alice sends $10 to Bob:

// For simplicity, balances are stored as strings parsed to integers, e.g. "30" for $30.
let alice_balance = parse_balance(db.get("alice.balance")?); // $30
if alice_balance < 10 {
    return Err("not enough money".into());
}

let bob_balance = parse_balance(db.get("bob.balance")?); // $0

// Another writer sets Alice's balance to $40

db.put("alice.balance", (alice_balance - 10).to_string())?; // Set to $20
db.put("bob.balance", (bob_balance + 10).to_string())?; // Set to $10

Never do anything like this! The code snippet has major correctness issues when there is more than a single writer. Alice's balance should have been $30 (40 - 10), but ended up being set to $20 (30 - 10). $10 is lost because another concurrent writer modified Alice's balance between get and put calls.

Transactions exist to solve exactly this kind of problem by providing a way to execute a sequence of operations in isolation, detecting conflicts between concurrent writers. RocksDB builds a transactional layer on top of versioning. Writes are staged in the transaction buffer and get applied to the database only after the transaction commits. Reading from the uncommitted transaction merges the transaction buffer with the rest of the tree. This way transactions can read their own writes.

Transactions in RocksDB come in optimistic and pessimistic flavors. The pessimistic flavor checks for conflicts between transactions when updating a key - calling put or delete. In the optimistic flavor, conflict checks are moved to commit time. The API for both flavors looks the same - the flavor is picked when opening the database as either TransactionDB or OptimisticTransactionDB:

let t1 = db.transaction();
t1.put("a", "1")?;
t1.get("a")?; // Returns "1" - the transaction reads its own uncommitted write.
t1.put("b", "1")?;
t1.put("c", "1")?;
t1.commit()?; // Updates to keys "a", "b" and "c" are applied to the memtable.

Pessimistic Transactions #

When using pessimistic transactions, keys involved in write operations are locked to perform conflict detection - the lock manager puts each updated key in a per-DB map, associating it with the transaction ID.

key_locks = {
    "a": T1,
    "b": T1,
    "c": T1,
}

If another transaction tries to update one of the locked keys concurrently, the conflicting transaction T2 blocks until T1 commits or rolls back - if that doesn't happen before the lock timeout, the operation returns an error:

let t2 = db.transaction();
// T1 has not committed yet.
t2.put("c", "2")?; // T2 is blocked waiting until it times out, or until T1 commits.
t2.put("d", "2")?;
// T1 committed.
t2.commit()?;

Locks are released when a transaction commits or rolls back. A third transaction, running at the same time as T1, but modifying a different set of keys, could proceed without being blocked.

By default, the conflict checks are done right when the operation is performed, which doesn't guarantee that no one else has updated the key since the transaction started:

let t1 = db.transaction();
{
    let t2 = db.transaction();
    t2.put("a", "2")?;
    t2.commit()?;
}
t1.put("a", "1")?; // Succeeds since T2 committed before this call.
t1.commit()?;

Setting a snapshot at the beginning of the transaction makes conflict detection catch updates made since the transaction started:

let mut opts = TransactionOptions::default();
// Setting a snapshot is the only difference compared to the previous example.
opts.set_snapshot(true);
let t1 = db.transaction_opt(&WriteOptions::default(), &opts);
{
    let t2 = db.transaction();
    t2.put("a", "2")?;
    t2.commit()?;
}
t1.put("a", "1")?; // Fails because T2 modified key "a" after the snapshot was set for T1.
t1.commit()?;

After T1 acquires a lock on key a, it checks whether the key was updated after the snapshot was set - whether there is a version of the key in the database with a sequence number higher than the snapshot sequence number. If there is, the write returns an error.

Optimistic Transactions #

Optimistic transactions don't check for conflicts on write operations, so put or delete never block or fail. Conflict checking happens on commits.

At a high level, commit-time validation is similar to how it's done in pessimistic transactions, but the implementation is more lightweight. Instead of tracking individual locked keys in a shared map, the lock manager hashes keys into 1M pre-allocated mutexes (yes, 1M is a lot - it trades off memory for reduced contention). Each operation on a key records the published sequence number at the time of the call.

While the mutexes are held for all keys involved in the commit, the transaction checks the memtable. If it contains a key with a sequence number larger than the recorded one, it means another transaction already committed the same key - the commit fails. The mutexes are released after all keys are validated and all updates are applied to the memtable.

Optimistic transactions limit validation to memtables (pessimistic transactions check the whole tree). If the memtable is flushed mid-transaction, there is nothing left to validate against, so the commit fails and the transaction has to be retried.

Mapping to Isolation Levels #

Isolation levels in databases define semantics of concurrent access - what happens when two concurrent transactions access the same data. I'll cover the most common levels used in modern databases. In SQL databases you can set the isolation level explicitly per transaction. There is no similar high-level API in RocksDB, but you can build different levels yourself with lower-level APIs.

Read uncommitted - the lowest isolation level, where the only guarantee is that concurrent transactions won't physically corrupt the database. This permits dirty reads - a transaction seeing uncommitted data from another transaction. Impossible in RocksDB because transaction writes sit in a buffer that gets applied to the database atomically on commit.

Read committed - a transaction sees only committed data, including data committed by other transactions after it started. You get this with a plain db.get, or when transactions don't use snapshots for reads. Two consecutive gets can see different values mid-transaction:

db.put("a", "1")?;
let t1 = db.transaction();
t1.get("a")?; // Returns "1"
{
    let t2 = db.transaction();
    t2.put("a", "2")?;
    t2.commit()?;
}
t1.get("a")?; // Returns "2"

Snapshot isolation - all reads in the transaction see a consistent snapshot of the database, and the transaction succeeds only if no updates it made conflict with updates made by other transactions since the snapshot was created. Can be achieved by enabling snapshots for both reads and write conflict detection - set_snapshot covers only conflict detection, and reads have to go through the transaction's snapshot(). You avoid non-repeatable reads, but the write skew anomaly is possible.

The snippet below illustrates the anomaly that happens when two transactions read the same data, then each updates a different key based on what it read. Alice has two bank accounts, $100 in each. The bank allows a single account to go negative, as long as the total across both accounts stays non-negative. Two concurrent transactions each withdraw $200 from a different account:

db.put("alice.checking", "100")?;
db.put("alice.savings", "100")?;

let mut opts = TransactionOptions::default();
opts.set_snapshot(true);

// T1 withdraws $200 from checking. T2 concurrently runs the same code,
// withdrawing $200 from savings.
let t1 = db.transaction_opt(&WriteOptions::default(), &opts);
let t2 = db.transaction_opt(&WriteOptions::default(), &opts);

let checking = parse_balance(t1.snapshot().get("alice.checking")?); // $100
let savings = parse_balance(t1.snapshot().get("alice.savings")?); // $100
if checking + savings - 200 >= 0 { // $100 + $100 - $200 = $0, the check passes.
    t1.put("alice.checking", (checking - 200).to_string())?; // Set to -$100.
}

// T2 passes the same check against its own snapshot and sets alice.savings to -$100.

// The transactions updated different keys, so conflict validation passes for both.
t1.commit()?;
t2.commit()?;

// Both accounts are now at -$100, the total is -$200.

Serializable - concurrent transactions run as if they were executed in serial order. You can get something close if you use get_for_update with snapshot isolation when transactions involve only point reads - you avoid dirty and non-repeatable reads and write skew. get_for_update is a special version of get used in transactions to avoid read-write conflicts e.g. when updating a key depends on the result of a read. get_for_update triggers conflict checks the same way as put and delete do. Here is the write skew example fixed by reading the balances with get_for_update, the rest of the code stays the same:

// Unlike snapshot reads, get_for_update reads participate in conflict detection.
let checking = parse_balance(t1.get_for_update("alice.checking", true)?); // $100
let savings = parse_balance(t1.get_for_update("alice.savings", true)?); // $100

// With pessimistic transactions, get_for_update blocks until it times out, then
// fails - the key is locked by T1. With optimistic transactions, the call
// succeeds and T2 aborts at commit.
t2.get_for_update("alice.checking", true)?;

The phantom reads anomaly is possible for transactions running range scans:

// Alice has a single account alice.checking.
db.put("alice.checking", "100")?;

let mut opts = TransactionOptions::default();
opts.set_snapshot(true);
let t1 = db.transaction_opt(&WriteOptions::default(), &opts);

// T1 computes the total across Alice's accounts, locking every key the scan returns.
let mut total = 0;
for item in t1.snapshot().iterator(IteratorMode::From(b"alice.", Direction::Forward)) {
    let (key, _) = item?;
    total += parse_balance(t1.get_for_update(key, true)?);
}
// total = $100.

// T2 opens a new account with a $50 deposit.
// The put doesn't conflict with T1 - T1 never read or locked alice.savings.
let t2 = db.transaction();
t2.put("alice.savings", "50")?;
t2.commit()?; // Succeeds.

t1.commit()?; // Succeeds.

T1 commits believing the total is $100, while Alice's accounts now hold $150. The new account is a phantom - get_for_update locked the keys the scan returned, but alice.savings didn't exist yet, so there was nothing to lock.

RocksDB has a range lock manager that locks key ranges instead of individual keys to prevent phantom reads. It was contributed in 2020, but it's not documented anywhere on the RocksDB wiki and not exposed through the C or Java APIs, so the feature is usable only from C++.

Fixing the Transfer #

With all we learned we can finally fix the $10 transfer bug between Alice and Bob. Transactions (either pessimistic or optimistic) with a snapshot set, using get_for_update instead of plain get, are what we need - another transaction can't change a balance between the read and the write without triggering a conflict:

let mut opts = TransactionOptions::default();
opts.set_snapshot(true);
let tx = db.transaction_opt(&WriteOptions::default(), &opts);

let Ok(alice_balance) = tx.get_for_update("alice.balance", true) else {
    // Can happen with pessimistic transactions.
    return Err("another transaction updated Alice's balance".into());
};
let alice_balance = parse_balance(alice_balance);
if alice_balance < 10 {
    return Err("not enough money".into());
}

let Ok(bob_balance) = tx.get_for_update("bob.balance", true) else {
    // Can happen with pessimistic transactions.
    return Err("another transaction updated Bob's balance".into());
};
let bob_balance = parse_balance(bob_balance);

tx.put("alice.balance", (alice_balance - 10).to_string())?;
tx.put("bob.balance", (bob_balance + 10).to_string())?;

if tx.commit().is_err() {
    // Can happen with optimistic transactions.
    return Err("another transaction updated Bob's or Alice's balance or the memtable was flushed".into());
}

Optimistic vs Pessimistic Performance #

From the user perspective, the only difference between pessimistic and optimistic transactions is where a transaction can abort on a conflict. Pessimistic transactions acquire locks on every write operation and abort early on conflicts (after exceeding the lock timeout). Optimistic transactions require no per-key locks on writes - coarse-grained locks are taken for the duration of a commit. Optimistic transactions typically provide higher throughput on workloads where conflicts between transactions are rare and where retrying the entire transaction on a conflict is cheap.

I benchmarked two workloads (source code is here) - one where pessimistic transactions win and one where optimistic transactions win.

In the "mostly read" workload, every transaction reads a shared key with get_for_update and writes a unique key - roughly one transaction in 1024 replaces the shared key instead. Starting from 2 threads, the throughput of optimistic transactions stays flat, while pessimistic transactions queue on the shared key's lock:

In the "contended key" workload, every transaction reads the same key with get_for_update, does CPU-bound work and writes the key back. Pessimistic transactions wait on the lock without consuming CPU, so the work runs once per commit. Optimistic transactions discover the conflict only at commit, after the work is done, redoing the expensive work on retry:

Who Uses RocksDB-native Transactions #

MyRocks - the MySQL storage engine built on RocksDB, and ArangoDB are the only large projects I'm aware of that rely on RocksDB transactions. Facebook's MyRocks repository was archived on GitHub on Mar 1, 2026, but the engine is still available as a part of MariaDB or Percona Server.

TiDB/TiKV and YugabyteDB, which run on RocksDB, don't use RocksDB-native transactions. CockroachDB's RocksDB-inspired key-value store Pebble doesn't support transactions at all. TiDB, YugabyteDB and CockroachDB implement their own distributed transactional layer above the key-value stores. The reason for this is that RocksDB-native transactions are node-local, i.e. the locks are held per key-value store instance running on a single node, and with a distributed database you have to coordinate transactions across multiple nodes.

Conclusion #

While LSM-trees give half of MVCC for free, there is a lot of practical complexity in implementing the other half. Concurrent algorithms are hard to get right, and making them efficient requires low-level techniques like using atomic operations on pointers. If you are implementing a database from scratch (I hope this blog post convinces you not to do that!), you are on your own with these techniques - in Rust, code built on raw pointers gets no protection from the borrow checker, and even memory-safe concurrent code paths covered entirely by the borrow checker are not free from logical races. The hard part is the concurrency, not the MVCC itself. Snapshots, atomic batches and conflict detection in both transaction flavors all come down to the same primitive - comparing sequence numbers.

Versioning Tables # Going back to GC, older key versions are removed by compactions, but how does the database know when an SST file or a memtable is safe to delete? What if there is an in-flight reader still accessing an already flushed memtable, or reading an SST that compaction is supposed to remove? RocksDB maintains a structure called SuperVersion, which is a reference-counted view of everything needed for reads: struct SuperVersion { active_memtable: ReferenceCounter<Memtable>, // memtable accepting writes immutable_memtables: Vec<ReferenceCounter<Memtable>>, // memtables ready to be flushed or just flushed ssts: ReferenceCounter<SstLevels>, // on-disk SSTs, each reference-counted individually } When a read starts, it acquires a reference to the structure. The reference is released only after the read is done. When the number of references to SuperVersion drops to zero, it releases its child fields, decrementing their reference counts. This way neither the structure itself nor any of its children can be freed mid-read. The cleanup - freeing the memtable memory or scheduling files for deletion is performed only when a child's own reference count reaches zero. The Wikipedia definition of MVCC says it's a "non-locking concurrency control method". In the database literature there is a distinction between locks and latches. Locks are logical - over keys, key ranges, or rows. When a lock is held on a key, nobody else can modify it until the lock is released. Latches are physical, e.g. mutexes that protect the database structures from data races during concurrent access. By this definition, the core of RocksDB doesn't take logical key locks, and the read and write paths are designed to not hold latches in the common hot cases. Some less frequently exercised code paths, however, do require latches - e.g. there is a coarse-grained per-DB mutex held briefly after a flush or compaction to replace the current SuperVersion. Transactions # Let's see how we can implement a classic balance transfer example with RocksDB, where Alice sends $10 to Bob: // For simplicity, balances are stored as strings parsed to integers, e.g. "30" for $30. let alice_balance = parse_balance(db.get("alice.balance")?); // $30 if alice_balance < 10 { return Err("not enough money".into()); } let bob_balance = parse_balance(db.get("bob.balance")?); // $0 // Another writer sets Alice's balance to $40 db.put("alice.balance", (alice_balance - 10).to_string())?; // Set to $20 db.put("bob.balance", (bob_balance + 10).to_string())?; // Set to $10 Never do anything like this! The code snippet has major correctness issues when there is more than a single writer. Alice's balance should have been $30 (40 - 10), but ended up being set to $20 (30 - 10). $10 is lost because another concurrent writer modified Alice's balance between get and put calls. Transactions exist to solve exactly this kind of problem by providing a way to execute a sequence of operations in isolation, detecting conflicts between concurrent writers. RocksDB builds a transactional layer on top of versioning. Writes are staged in the transaction buffer and get applied to the database only after the transaction commits. Reading from the uncommitted transaction merges the transaction buffer with the rest of the tree. This way transactions can read their own writes. Transactions in RocksDB come in optimistic and pessimistic flavors. The pessimistic flavor checks for conflicts between transactions when updating a key - calling put or delete. In the optimistic flavor, conflict checks are moved to commit time. The API for both flavors looks the same - the flavor is picked when opening the database as either TransactionDB or OptimisticTransactionDB: let t1 = db.transaction(); t1.put("a", "1")?; t1.get("a")?; // Returns "1" - the transaction reads its own uncommitted write. t1.put("b", "1")?; t1.put("c", "1")?; t1.commit()?; // Updates to keys "a", "b" and "c" are applied to the memtable. Pessimistic Transactions # When using pessimistic transactions, keys involved in write operations are locked to perform conflict detection - the lock manager puts each updated key in a per-DB map, associating it with the transaction ID. key_locks = { "a": T1, "b": T1, "c": T1, } If another transaction tries to update one of the locked keys concurrently, the conflicting transaction T2 blocks until T1 commits or rolls back - if that doesn't happen before the lock timeout, the operation returns an error: let t2 = db.transaction(); // T1 has not committed yet. t2.put("c", "2")?; // T2 is blocked waiting until it times out, or until T1 commits. t2.put("d", "2")?; // T1 committed. t2.commit()?; Locks are released when a transaction commits or rolls back. A third transaction, running at the same time as T1, but modifying a different set of keys, could proceed without being blocked. By default, the conflict checks are done right when the operation is performed, which doesn't guarantee that no one else has updated the key since the transaction started: let t1 = db.transaction(); { let t2 = db.transaction(); t2.put("a", "2")?; t2.commit()?; } t1.put("a", "1")?; // Succeeds since T2 committed before this call. t1.commit()?; Setting a snapshot at the beginning of the transaction makes conflict detection catch updates made since the transaction started: let mut opts = TransactionOptions::default(); // Setting a snapshot is the only difference compared to the previous example. opts.set_snapshot(true); let t1 = db.transaction_opt(&WriteOptions::default(), &opts); { let t2 = db.transaction(); t2.put("a", "2")?; t2.commit()?; } t1.put("a", "1")?; // Fails because T2 modified key "a" after the snapshot was set for T1. t1.commit()?; After T1 acquires a lock on key a, it checks whether the key was updated after the snapshot was set - whether there is a version of the key in the database with a sequence number higher than the snapshot sequence number. If there is, the write returns an error. Optimistic Transactions # Optimistic transactions don't check for conflicts on write operations, so put or delete never block or fail. Conflict checking happens on commits. At a high level, commit-time validation is similar to how it's done in pessimistic transactions, but the implementation is more lightweight. Instead of tracking individual locked keys in a shared map, the lock manager hashes keys into 1M pre-allocated mutexes (yes, 1M is a lot - it trades off memory for reduced contention). Each operation on a key records the published sequence number at the time of the call. While the mutexes are held for all keys involved in the commit, the transaction checks the memtable. If it contains a key with a sequence number larger than the recorded one, it means another transaction already committed the same key - the commit fails. The mutexes are released after all keys are validated and all updates are applied to the memtable. Optimistic transactions limit validation to memtables (pessimistic transactions check the whole tree). If the memtable is flushed mid-transaction, there is nothing left to validate against, so the commit fails and the transaction has to be retried. Mapping to Isolation Levels # Isolation levels in databases define semantics of concurrent access - what happens when two concurrent transactions access the same data. I'll cover the most common levels used in modern databases. In SQL databases you can set the isolation level explicitly per transaction. There is no similar high-level API in RocksDB, but you can build different levels yourself with lower-level APIs. Read uncommitted - the lowest isolation level, where the only guarantee is that concurrent transactions won't physically corrupt the database. This permits dirty reads - a transaction seeing uncommitted data from another transaction. Impossible in RocksDB because transaction writes sit in a buffer that gets applied to the database atomically on commit. Read committed - a transaction sees only committed data, including data committed by other transactions after it started. You get this with a plain db.get, or when transactions don't use snapshots for reads. Two consecutive gets can see different values mid-transaction: db.put("a", "1")?; let t1 = db.transaction(); t1.get("a")?; // Returns "1" { let t2 = db.transaction(); t2.put("a", "2")?; t2.commit()?; } t1.get("a")?; // Returns "2" Snapshot isolation - all reads in the transaction see a consistent snapshot of the database, and the transaction succeeds only if no updates it made conflict with updates made by other transactions since the snapshot was created. Can be achieved by enabling snapshots for both reads and write conflict detection - set_snapshot covers only conflict detection, and reads have to go through the transaction's snapshot(). You avoid non-repeatable reads, but the write skew anomaly is possible. The snippet below illustrates the anomaly that happens when two transactions read the same data, then each updates a different key based on what it read. Alice has two bank accounts, $100 in each. The bank allows a single account to go negative, as long as the total across both accounts stays non-negative. Two concurrent transactions each withdraw $200 from a different account: db.put("alice.checking", "100")?; db.put("alice.savings", "100")?; let mut opts = TransactionOptions::default(); opts.set_snapshot(true); // T1 withdraws $200 from checking. T2 concurrently runs the same code, // withdrawing $200 from savings. let t1 = db.transaction_opt(&WriteOptions::default(), &opts); let t2 = db.transaction_opt(&WriteOptions::default(), &opts); let checking = parse_balance(t1.snapshot().get("alice.checking")?); // $100 let savings = parse_balance(t1.snapshot().get("alice.savings")?); // $100 if checking + savings - 200 >= 0 { // $100 + $100 - $200 = $0, the check passes. t1.put("alice.checking", (checking - 200).to_string())?; // Set to -$100. } // T2 passes the same check against its own snapshot and sets alice.savings to -$100. // The transactions updated different keys, so conflict validation passes for both. t1.commit()?; t2.commit()?; // Both accounts are now at -$100, the total is -$200. Serializable - concurrent transactions run as if they were executed in serial order. You can get something close if you use get_for_update with snapshot isolation when transactions involve only point reads - you avoid dirty and non-repeatable reads and write skew. get_for_update is a special version of get used in transactions to avoid read-write conflicts e.g. when updating a key depends on the result of a read. get_for_update triggers conflict checks the same way as put and delete do. Here is the write skew example fixed by reading the balances with get_for_update, the rest of the code stays the same: // Unlike snapshot reads, get_for_update reads participate in conflict detection. let checking = parse_balance(t1.get_for_update("alice.checking", true)?); // $100 let savings = parse_balance(t1.get_for_update("alice.savings", true)?); // $100 // With pessimistic transactions, get_for_update blocks until it times out, then // fails - the key is locked by T1. With optimistic transactions, the call // succeeds and T2 aborts at commit. t2.get_for_update("alice.checking", true)?; The phantom reads anomaly is possible for transactions running range scans: // Alice has a single account alice.checking. db.put("alice.checking", "100")?; let mut opts = TransactionOptions::default(); opts.set_snapshot(true); let t1 = db.transaction_opt(&WriteOptions::default(), &opts); // T1 computes the total across Alice's accounts, locking every key the scan returns. let mut total = 0; for item in t1.snapshot().iterator(IteratorMode::From(b"alice.", Direction::Forward)) { let (key, _) = item?; total += parse_balance(t1.get_for_update(key, true)?); } // total = $100. // T2 opens a new account with a $50 deposit. // The put doesn't conflict with T1 - T1 never read or locked alice.savings. let t2 = db.transaction(); t2.put("alice.savings", "50")?; t2.commit()?; // Succeeds. t1.commit()?; // Succeeds. T1 commits believing the total is $100, while Alice's accounts now hold $150. The new account is a phantom - get_for_update locked the keys the scan returned, but alice.savings didn't exist yet, so there was nothing to lock. RocksDB has a range lock manager that locks key ranges instead of individual keys to prevent phantom reads. It was contributed in 2020, but it's not documented anywhere on the RocksDB wiki and not exposed through the C or Java APIs, so the feature is usable only from C++. Fixing the Transfer # With all we learned we can finally fix the $10 transfer bug between Alice and Bob. Transactions (either pessimistic or optimistic) with a snapshot set, using get_for_update instead of plain get, are what we need - another transaction can't change a balance between the read and the write without triggering a conflict: let mut opts = TransactionOptions::default(); opts.set_snapshot(true); let tx = db.transaction_opt(&WriteOptions::default(), &opts); let Ok(alice_balance) = tx.get_for_update("alice.balance", true) else { // Can happen with pessimistic transactions. return Err("another transaction updated Alice's balance".into()); }; let alice_balance = parse_balance(alice_balance); if alice_balance < 10 { return Err("not enough money".into()); } let Ok(bob_balance) = tx.get_for_update("bob.balance", true) else { // Can happen with pessimistic transactions. return Err("another transaction updated Bob's balance".into()); }; let bob_balance = parse_balance(bob_balance); tx.put("alice.balance", (alice_balance - 10).to_string())?; tx.put("bob.balance", (bob_balance + 10).to_string())?; if tx.commit().is_err() { // Can happen with optimistic transactions. return Err("another transaction updated Bob's or Alice's balance or the memtable was flushed".into()); } Optimistic vs Pessimistic Performance # From the user perspective, the only difference between pessimistic and optimistic transactions is where a transaction can abort on a conflict. Pessimistic transactions acquire locks on every write operation and abort early on conflicts (after exceeding the lock timeout). Optimistic transactions require no per-key locks on writes - coarse-grained locks are taken for the duration of a commit. Optimistic transactions typically provide higher throughput on workloads where conflicts between transactions are rare and where retrying the entire transaction on a conflict is cheap. I benchmarked two workloads (source code is here) - one where pessimistic transactions win and one where optimistic transactions win. In the "mostly read" workload, every transaction reads a shared key with get_for_update and writes a unique key - roughly one transaction in 1024 replaces the shared key instead. Starting from 2 threads, the throughput of optimistic transactions stays flat, while pessimistic transactions queue on the shared key's lock: In the "contended key" workload, every transaction reads the same key with get_for_update, does CPU-bound work and writes the key back. Pessimistic transactions wait on the lock without consuming CPU, so the work runs once per commit. Optimistic transactions discover the conflict only at commit, after the work is done, redoing the expensive work on retry: Who Uses RocksDB-native Transactions # MyRocks - the MySQL storage engine built on RocksDB, and ArangoDB are the only large projects I'm aware of that rely on RocksDB transactions. Facebook's MyRocks repository was archived on GitHub on Mar 1, 2026, but the engine is still available as a part of MariaDB or Percona Server. TiDB/TiKV and YugabyteDB, which run on RocksDB, don't use RocksDB-native transactions. CockroachDB's RocksDB-inspired key-value store Pebble doesn't support transactions at all. TiDB, YugabyteDB and CockroachDB implement their own distributed transactional layer above the key-value stores. The reason for this is that RocksDB-native transactions are node-local, i.e. the locks are held per key-value store instance running on a single node, and with a distributed database you have to coordinate transactions across multiple nodes. Conclusion # While LSM-trees give half of MVCC for free, there is a lot of practical complexity in implementing the other half. Concurrent algorithms are hard to get right, and making them efficient requires low-level techniques like using atomic operations on pointers. If you are implementing a database from scratch (I hope this blog post convinces you not to do that!), you are on your own with these techniques - in Rust, code built on raw pointers gets no protection from the borrow checker, and even memory-safe concurrent code paths covered entirely by the borrow checker are not free from logical races. The hard part is the concurrency, not the MVCC itself. Snapshots, atomic batches and conflict detection in both transaction flavors all come down to the same primitive - comparing sequence numbers.

blocks.html_en: RocksDB is built on an LSM-tree, which never modifies data in-place - every write creates a new version of the key. That's half of what you need to implement Multi-Version Concurrency Control (MVCC).

blocks.html_en: Real-world databases serve many clients reading and writing the same data at the same time. The simplest way to make concurrent access safe is locking (think of a hash map protected with a mutex). This works from the correctness perspective, but makes readers and writers block each other, which can quickly become a performance bottleneck. MVCC solves this problem, allowing readers and writers to proceed concurrently. The idea is that:

blocks.html_en: All inserts, updates and deletes go through a write buffer (memtable) and then get flushed to disk into an immutable SST (Static Sorted Table) file - you naturally have multiple versions of the same key. Compaction is the garbage collection process that merges SSTs and removes older versions of keys.

You also need a way to provide readers a snapshot of the database, and the GC process needs some way of knowing which tables are being accessed by in-flight queries.

blocks.html_en: Internally, RocksDB assigns each inserted key a monotonically increasing number, which it calls a sequence number. When you insert a key into RocksDB, what actually gets into the memtable and SST files on disk is a triplet of (user_key, sequence_number, value_type), stored together with the value. The user key is the key you provided, the sequence number is a counter incremented on every write, and finally the value type is whether it's an insert or a delete (or other operations, which are irrelevant for this blog post).

blocks.html_en: RocksDB tracks two sequence numbers - the last allocated sequence (the last number assigned on write), and the published sequence (the number visible to readers, advanced after a write completes).

The reader R, when it sees dog,b with the sequence number 5, will skip over it, as it is allowed to see only keys with a sequence number of 4 or below.

sentences.zh: 当读取查询开始时，RocksDB 会捕获已发布的序列号。

sentences.en: This sequence is then used for querying the memtables and the SSTs on disk.
sentences.zh: 然后，该序列用于查询磁盘上的内存表和 SST。

sentences.en: The reader R, when it sees dog,b with the sequence number 5, will skip over it, as it is allowed to see only keys with a sequence number of 4 or below.
sentences.zh: 当阅读器R看到序列号为5的dog,b时，将跳过它，因为它只允许看到序列号为4或以下的键。

blocks.html_en: The memtable implementation in RocksDB is not that far from our toy example. As in the example we used, it's based on a linked list, specifically on a skip list. The data structure adds layers of links that allow fast search and insertion in sorted order:

sentences.en: The memtable implementation in RocksDB is not that far from our toy example.
sentences.zh: RocksDB 中的 memtable 实现与我们的玩具示例相差不远。

sentences.en: As in the example we used, it's based on a linked list, specifically on a skip list.
sentences.zh: 正如我们使用的示例中一样，它基于链接列表，特别是跳跃列表。

sentences.en: The data structure adds layers of links that allow fast search and insertion in sorted order:
sentences.zh: 该数据结构添加了链接层，允许按排序顺序快速搜索和插入：

blocks.html_en: Layer 0 contains every entry - like the linked list from the example before. Each next layer contains a subset of the previous layer's entries. The search starts at the sparsest layer, descending to the full linked list. This structure gives O(log n) average time complexity for search.

sentences.en: Layer 0 contains every entry - like the linked list from the example before.
sentences.zh: 第 0 层包含每个条目 - 就像前面示例中的链接列表一样。

sentences.en: Each next layer contains a subset of the previous layer's entries.
sentences.zh: 下一层包含上一层条目的子集。

sentences.en: The search starts at the sparsest layer, descending to the full linked list.
sentences.zh: 搜索从最稀疏的层开始，下降到完整的链表。

sentences.en: This structure gives O(log n) average time complexity for search.
sentences.zh: 该结构给出了 O(log n) 平均搜索时间复杂度。

blocks.html_en: When comparing internal keys, RocksDB first orders by the user-provided key ascending and then by the sequence number descending, so that when two keys share the same user key, the one with the larger sequence number comes first. A read query that captured the published sequence number 4 translates into a skip list seek to dog 4. The seek starts with cat 3 at layer 1, skips to dog 5 on layer 1, descends to dog 5 on layer 0 and finally lands on dog 1. The search can stop there since in the internal key order dog 4 is larger than dog 5, but smaller than dog 1. In Rust this could be expressed with the Ord trait this way:

sentences.en: When comparing internal keys, RocksDB first orders by the user-provided key ascending and then by the sequence number descending, so that when two keys share the same user key, the one with the larger sequence number comes first.
sentences.zh: 在比较内部密钥时，RocksDB 首先按用户提供的密钥升序排序，然后按序列号降序排序，这样当两个密钥共享相同的用户密钥时，序列号较大的排在前面。

sentences.en: A read query that captured the published sequence number 4 translates into a skip list seek to dog 4.
sentences.zh: 捕获已发布序列号 4 的读取查询会转换为跟踪 4 的跳跃列表搜索。

sentences.en: The seek starts with cat 3 at layer 1, skips to dog 5 on layer 1, descends to dog 5 on layer 0 and finally lands on dog 1.
sentences.zh: 搜索从第 1 层的猫 3 开始，跳到第 1 层的狗 5，下降到第 0 层的狗 5，最后到达狗 1。

sentences.en: The search can stop there since in the internal key order dog 4 is larger than dog 5, but smaller than dog 1.
sentences.zh: 搜索可以在那里停止，因为在内部键顺序中，dog 4 大于dog 5，但小于dog 1。

sentences.en: In Rust this could be expressed with the Ord trait this way:
sentences.zh: 在 Rust 中，这可以用 Ord 特征来表达：

blocks.html_en: The skip list in RocksDB is concurrent, meaning it doesn't require locking, so reads and writes can truly happen concurrently. The implementation uses atomic operations on pointers. Garbage collection in concurrent algorithms in non-GC languages is a hard problem, which RocksDB solves elegantly by relying on an arena allocator - individual entries are never freed, deletes are inserts with a DELETE value type and the memory for the entire memtable is freed after the memtable is no longer in use.

sentences.en: The skip list in RocksDB is concurrent, meaning it doesn't require locking, so reads and writes can truly happen concurrently.
sentences.zh: RocksDB中的跳跃列表是并发的，这意味着它不需要锁定，因此读取和写入可以真正并发发生。

sentences.en: The implementation uses atomic operations on pointers.
sentences.zh: 该实现对指针使用原子操作。

sentences.en: Garbage collection in concurrent algorithms in non-GC languages is a hard problem, which RocksDB solves elegantly by relying on an arena allocator - individual entries are never freed, deletes are inserts with a DELETE value type and the memory for the entire memtable is freed after the memtable is no longer in use.
sentences.zh: 非 GC 语言的并发算法中的垃圾收集是一个难题，RocksDB 通过依赖 arena 分配器优雅地解决了这个问题 - 单个条目永远不会被释放，删除是使用 DELETE 值类型进行插入，并且在不再使用 memtable 后释放整个 memtable 的内存。

blocks.html_en: Atomic Updates #

sentences.en: Atomic Updates #
sentences.zh: 原子更新#

blocks.html_en: The write batch API provides atomicity - keys written in the same batch become visible together:

sentences.en: The write batch API provides atomicity - keys written in the same batch become visible together:
sentences.zh: 批量写入 API 提供原子性 - 在同一批次中写入的密钥一起变得可见：

blocks.html_en: Atomicity becomes straightforward to implement with the key versioning and sequencing mechanism we discussed above. When the batch is applied to the database (the db.write call), sequence numbers for all keys are allocated atomically by incrementing the sequence number by 3 (the number of keys in the batch) and then publishing that number only after all three keys are inserted into the memtable. This ensures no reader can see a partial batch - they either see all three keys, or none of them.

sentences.en: Atomicity becomes straightforward to implement with the key versioning and sequencing mechanism we discussed above.
sentences.zh: 通过我们上面讨论的关键版本控制和排序机制，原子性变得很容易实现。

sentences.en: When the batch is applied to the database (the db.write call), sequence numbers for all keys are allocated atomically by incrementing the sequence number by 3 (the number of keys in the batch) and then publishing that number only after all three keys are inserted into the memtable.
sentences.zh: 当批处理应用于数据库时（db.write 调用），所有键的序列号都会以原子方式分配，方法是将序列号增加 3（批处理中的键数），然后仅在所有三个键都插入内存表后才发布该数字。

sentences.en: This ensures no reader can see a partial batch - they either see all three keys, or none of them.
sentences.zh: 这确保没有读者可以看到部分批次 - 他们要么看到所有三个键，要么看不到任何一个。

blocks.html_en: Snapshots #

sentences.en: Snapshots #
sentences.zh: 快照#

blocks.html_en: Every get implicitly captures the latest published sequence number. The snapshot API pins it explicitly, so multiple reads can see the database at the same point in time:

sentences.en: Every get implicitly captures the latest published sequence number.
sentences.zh: 每次获取都会隐式捕获最新发布的序列号。

sentences.en: The snapshot API pins it explicitly, so multiple reads can see the database at the same point in time:
sentences.zh: 快照 API 显式固定它，因此多次读取可以在同一时间点查看数据库：

blocks.html_en: A snapshot is just the published sequence number recorded when db.snapshot() is called - reads through the snapshot skip keys with higher sequence numbers. RocksDB also registers the snapshot in a list of active snapshots. Compaction checks the list and drops an older version of a key only if no live snapshot can still see it.

sentences.en: A snapshot is just the published sequence number recorded when db.snapshot() is called - reads through the snapshot skip keys with higher sequence numbers.
sentences.zh: 快照只是调用 db.snapshot() 时记录的已发布序列号 - 读取具有更高序列号的快照跳过键。

sentences.en: RocksDB also registers the snapshot in a list of active snapshots.
sentences.zh: RocksDB 还将快照注册到活动快照列表中。

sentences.en: Compaction checks the list and drops an older version of a key only if no live snapshot can still see it.
sentences.zh: 仅当没有实时快照仍然可以看到旧版本的密钥时，压缩才会检查列表并删除旧版本的密钥。

blocks.html_en: Versioning Tables #

sentences.en: Versioning Tables #
sentences.zh: 版本控制表#

blocks.html_en: Going back to GC, older key versions are removed by compactions, but how does the database know when an SST file or a memtable is safe to delete? What if there is an in-flight reader still accessing an already flushed memtable, or reading an SST that compaction is supposed to remove?

sentences.en: Going back to GC, older key versions are removed by compactions, but how does the database know when an SST file or a memtable is safe to delete?
sentences.zh: 回到 GC，旧的密钥版本会通过压缩删除，但是数据库如何知道 SST 文件或内存表何时可以安全删除？

sentences.en: What if there is an in-flight reader still accessing an already flushed memtable, or reading an SST that compaction is supposed to remove?
sentences.zh: 如果有一个正在运行的读取器仍在访问已刷新的内存表，或读取压缩应删除的 SST，该怎么办？

blocks.html_en: RocksDB maintains a structure called SuperVersion, which is a reference-counted view of everything needed for reads:

sentences.en: RocksDB maintains a structure called SuperVersion, which is a reference-counted view of everything needed for reads:
sentences.zh: RocksDB 维护一个名为 SuperVersion 的结构，它是读取所需的所有内容的引用计数视图：

blocks.html_en: When a read starts, it acquires a reference to the structure. The reference is released only after the read is done. When the number of references to SuperVersion drops to zero, it releases its child fields, decrementing their reference counts. This way neither the structure itself nor any of its children can be freed mid-read. The cleanup - freeing the memtable memory or scheduling files for deletion is performed only when a child's own reference count reaches zero.

sentences.en: When a read starts, it acquires a reference to the structure.
sentences.zh: 当读取开始时，它获取对该结构的引用。

sentences.en: The reference is released only after the read is done.
sentences.zh: 仅在读取完成后才会释放引用。

sentences.en: When the number of references to SuperVersion drops to zero, it releases its child fields, decrementing their reference counts.
sentences.zh: 当对 SuperVersion 的引用数量降至零时，它会释放其子字段，从而减少它们的引用计数。

sentences.en: This way neither the structure itself nor any of its children can be freed mid-read.
sentences.zh: 这样，结构本身及其任何子结构都不能在读取过程中被释放。

sentences.en: The cleanup - freeing the memtable memory or scheduling files for deletion is performed only when a child's own reference count reaches zero.
sentences.zh: 仅当子进程自己的引用计数达到零时，才会执行清理 - 释放内存表内存或安排文件删除。

blocks.html_en: The Wikipedia definition of MVCC says it's a "non-locking concurrency control method". In the database literature there is a distinction between locks and latches. Locks are logical - over keys, key ranges, or rows. When a lock is held on a key, nobody else can modify it until the lock is released. Latches are physical, e.g. mutexes that protect the database structures from data races during concurrent access.

sentences.en: The Wikipedia definition of MVCC says it's a "non-locking concurrency control method".
sentences.zh: 维基百科对MVCC的定义说它是一种“非锁定并发控制方法”

sentences.en: In the database literature there is a distinction between locks and latches.
sentences.zh: 在数据库文献中，锁和闩锁之间存在区别。

sentences.en: Locks are logical - over keys, key ranges, or rows.
sentences.zh: 锁是逻辑锁——针对键、键范围或行。

sentences.en: When a lock is held on a key, nobody else can modify it until the lock is released.
sentences.zh: 当某个键被锁定时，在该锁被释放之前，没有其他人可以修改它。

sentences.en: Latches are physical, e.g. mutexes that protect the database structures from data races during concurrent access.
sentences.zh: 锁存器是物理的，例如互斥体可保护数据库结构在并发访问期间免受数据竞争的影响。

blocks.html_en: By this definition, the core of RocksDB doesn't take logical key locks, and the read and write paths are designed to not hold latches in the common hot cases. Some less frequently exercised code paths, however, do require latches - e.g. there is a coarse-grained per-DB mutex held briefly after a flush or compaction to replace the current SuperVersion.

sentences.en: By this definition, the core of RocksDB doesn't take logical key locks, and the read and write paths are designed to not hold latches in the common hot cases.
sentences.zh: 根据这个定义，RocksDB 的核心不采用逻辑键锁，并且读写路径被设计为在常见的热点情况下不持有锁存器。

sentences.en: Some less frequently exercised code paths, however, do require latches - e.g. there is a coarse-grained per-DB mutex held briefly after a flush or compaction to replace the current SuperVersion.
sentences.zh: 然而，一些不常使用的代码路径确实需要锁存器 - 例如在刷新或压缩后会短暂保留一个粗粒度的每数据库互斥锁以替换当前的超级版本。

blocks.html_en: Transactions #

sentences.en: Transactions #
sentences.zh: 交易#

blocks.html_en: Let's see how we can implement a classic balance transfer example with RocksDB, where Alice sends $10 to Bob:

sentences.en: Let's see how we can implement a classic balance transfer example with RocksDB, where Alice sends $10 to Bob:
sentences.zh: 让我们看看如何使用 RocksDB 实现一个经典的余额转账示例，其中 Alice 向 Bob 发送了 10 美元：

blocks.html_en: Never do anything like this! The code snippet has major correctness issues when there is more than a single writer. Alice's balance should have been $30 (40 - 10), but ended up being set to $20 (30 - 10). $10 is lost because another concurrent writer modified Alice's balance between get and put calls.

sentences.en: Never do anything like this!
sentences.zh: 永远不要做这样的事！

sentences.en: The code snippet has major correctness issues when there is more than a single writer.
sentences.zh: 当有多个编写者时，代码片段存在严重的正确性问题。

sentences.en: Alice's balance should have been $30 (40 - 10), but ended up being set to $20 (30 - 10). $10 is lost because another concurrent writer modified Alice's balance between get and put calls.
sentences.zh: Alice 的余额本应为 30 美元 (40 - 10)，但最终被设置为 20 美元 (30 - 10)。由于另一位并发作者修改了 Alice 在 get 和 put 调用之间的余额，因此损失了 10 美元。

blocks.html_en: Transactions exist to solve exactly this kind of problem by providing a way to execute a sequence of operations in isolation, detecting conflicts between concurrent writers. RocksDB builds a transactional layer on top of versioning. Writes are staged in the transaction buffer and get applied to the database only after the transaction commits. Reading from the uncommitted transaction merges the transaction buffer with the rest of the tree. This way transactions can read their own writes.

sentences.en: Transactions exist to solve exactly this kind of problem by providing a way to execute a sequence of operations in isolation, detecting conflicts between concurrent writers.
sentences.zh: 事务的存在正是为了解决此类问题，它提供了一种独立执行一系列操作的方法，检测并发写入者之间的冲突。

sentences.en: RocksDB builds a transactional layer on top of versioning.
sentences.zh: RocksDB 在版本控制之上构建了一个事务层。

sentences.en: Writes are staged in the transaction buffer and get applied to the database only after the transaction commits.
sentences.zh: 写操作暂存在事务缓冲区中，并且仅在事务提交后才应用于数据库。

sentences.en: Reading from the uncommitted transaction merges the transaction buffer with the rest of the tree.
sentences.zh: 从未提交的事务中读取数据会将事务缓冲区与树的其余部分合并。

sentences.en: This way transactions can read their own writes.
sentences.zh: 这样事务就可以读取自己的写入。

blocks.html_en: Transactions in RocksDB come in optimistic and pessimistic flavors. The pessimistic flavor checks for conflicts between transactions when updating a key - calling put or delete. In the optimistic flavor, conflict checks are moved to commit time. The API for both flavors looks the same - the flavor is picked when opening the database as either TransactionDB or OptimisticTransactionDB:

sentences.en: Transactions in RocksDB come in optimistic and pessimistic flavors.
sentences.zh: RocksDB 中的事务有乐观和悲观两种类型。

sentences.en: The pessimistic flavor checks for conflicts between transactions when updating a key - calling put or delete.
sentences.zh: 悲观风格在更新键时检查事务之间的冲突 - 调用 put 或 delete。

sentences.en: In the optimistic flavor, conflict checks are moved to commit time.
sentences.zh: 在乐观的风格中，冲突检查被移至提交时间。

sentences.en: The API for both flavors looks the same - the flavor is picked when opening the database as either TransactionDB or OptimisticTransactionDB:
sentences.zh: 两种风格的 API 看起来相同 - 在以 TransactionDB 或 OptimisticTransactionDB 打开数据库时选择风格：

blocks.html_en: Pessimistic Transactions #

sentences.en: Pessimistic Transactions #
sentences.zh: 悲观交易#

blocks.html_en: When using pessimistic transactions, keys involved in write operations are locked to perform conflict detection - the lock manager puts each updated key in a per-DB map, associating it with the transaction ID.

sentences.en: When using pessimistic transactions, keys involved in write operations are locked to perform conflict detection - the lock manager puts each updated key in a per-DB map, associating it with the transaction ID.
sentences.zh: 使用悲观事务时，写入操作中涉及的键将被锁定以执行冲突检测 - 锁管理器将每个更新的键放入每个数据库映射中，并将其与事务 ID 相关联。

blocks.html_en: If another transaction tries to update one of the locked keys concurrently, the conflicting transaction T2 blocks until T1 commits or rolls back - if that doesn't happen before the lock timeout, the operation returns an error:

sentences.en: If another transaction tries to update one of the locked keys concurrently, the conflicting transaction T2 blocks until T1 commits or rolls back - if that doesn't happen before the lock timeout, the operation returns an error:
sentences.zh: 如果另一个事务尝试同时更新其中一个锁定的键，则冲突的事务 T2 将阻塞，直到 T1 提交或回滚 - 如果在锁定超时之前没有发生这种情况，则该操作将返回错误：

blocks.html_en: Locks are released when a transaction commits or rolls back. A third transaction, running at the same time as T1, but modifying a different set of keys, could proceed without being blocked.

sentences.en: Locks are released when a transaction commits or rolls back.
sentences.zh: 当事务提交或回滚时，锁被释放。

sentences.en: A third transaction, running at the same time as T1, but modifying a different set of keys, could proceed without being blocked.
sentences.zh: 第三个事务与 T1 同时运行，但修改了一组不同的密钥，可以继续进行而不会被阻止。

blocks.html_en: By default, the conflict checks are done right when the operation is performed, which doesn't guarantee that no one else has updated the key since the transaction started:

sentences.en: By default, the conflict checks are done right when the operation is performed, which doesn't guarantee that no one else has updated the key since the transaction started:
sentences.zh: 默认情况下，冲突检查是在执行操作时完成的，这不能保证自事务开始以来没有其他人更新过密钥：

blocks.html_en: Setting a snapshot at the beginning of the transaction makes conflict detection catch updates made since the transaction started:

sentences.en: Setting a snapshot at the beginning of the transaction makes conflict detection catch updates made since the transaction started:
sentences.zh: 在事务开始时设置快照可以使冲突检测捕获自事务启动以来所做的更新：

blocks.html_en: After T1 acquires a lock on key a, it checks whether the key was updated after the snapshot was set - whether there is a version of the key in the database with a sequence number higher than the snapshot sequence number. If there is, the write returns an error.

sentences.en: After T1 acquires a lock on key a, it checks whether the key was updated after the snapshot was set - whether there is a version of the key in the database with a sequence number higher than the snapshot sequence number.
sentences.zh: T1 获取密钥 a 的锁后，会检查该密钥在设置快照后是否已更新——数据库中是否存在序列号高于快照序列号的密钥版本。

sentences.en: If there is, the write returns an error.
sentences.zh: 如果存在，写入将返回错误。

blocks.html_en: Optimistic Transactions #

sentences.en: Optimistic Transactions #
sentences.zh: 乐观交易#

blocks.html_en: Optimistic transactions don't check for conflicts on write operations, so put or delete never block or fail. Conflict checking happens on commits.

sentences.en: Optimistic transactions don't check for conflicts on write operations, so put or delete never block or fail.
sentences.zh: 乐观事务不会检查写入操作上的冲突，因此放置或删除永远不会阻塞或失败。

sentences.en: Conflict checking happens on commits.
sentences.zh: 冲突检查发生在提交上。

blocks.html_en: At a high level, commit-time validation is similar to how it's done in pessimistic transactions, but the implementation is more lightweight. Instead of tracking individual locked keys in a shared map, the lock manager hashes keys into 1M pre-allocated mutexes (yes, 1M is a lot - it trades off memory for reduced contention). Each operation on a key records the published sequence number at the time of the call.

sentences.en: At a high level, commit-time validation is similar to how it's done in pessimistic transactions, but the implementation is more lightweight.
sentences.zh: 在较高的层面上，提交时验证与悲观事务中的完成方式类似，但实现更加轻量级。

sentences.en: Instead of tracking individual locked keys in a shared map, the lock manager hashes keys into 1M pre-allocated mutexes (yes, 1M is a lot - it trades off memory for reduced contention).
sentences.zh: 锁管理器不是在共享映射中跟踪各个锁定的密钥，而是将密钥散列到 1M 预分配的互斥体中（是的，1M 很大 - 它会牺牲内存以减少争用）。

sentences.en: Each operation on a key records the published sequence number at the time of the call.
sentences.zh: 对键的每次操作都会记录调用时发布的序列号。

blocks.html_en: While the mutexes are held for all keys involved in the commit, the transaction checks the memtable. If it contains a key with a sequence number larger than the recorded one, it means another transaction already committed the same key - the commit fails. The mutexes are released after all keys are validated and all updates are applied to the memtable.

sentences.en: While the mutexes are held for all keys involved in the commit, the transaction checks the memtable.
sentences.zh: 虽然提交中涉及的所有键都保留了互斥锁，但事务会检查内存表。

sentences.en: If it contains a key with a sequence number larger than the recorded one, it means another transaction already committed the same key - the commit fails.
sentences.zh: 如果它包含的键的序列号大于记录的序列号，则意味着另一个事务已经提交了相同的键 - 提交失败。

sentences.en: The mutexes are released after all keys are validated and all updates are applied to the memtable.
sentences.zh: 在验证所有密钥并将所有更新应用于内存表后，将释放互斥体。

blocks.html_en: Optimistic transactions limit validation to memtables (pessimistic transactions check the whole tree). If the memtable is flushed mid-transaction, there is nothing left to validate against, so the commit fails and the transaction has to be retried.

sentences.en: Optimistic transactions limit validation to memtables (pessimistic transactions check the whole tree).
sentences.zh: 乐观事务限制对内存表的验证（悲观事务检查整个树）。

sentences.en: If the memtable is flushed mid-transaction, there is nothing left to validate against, so the commit fails and the transaction has to be retried.
sentences.zh: 如果内存表在事务中被刷新，则没有任何内容可供验证，因此提交失败并且必须重试事务。

blocks.html_en: Mapping to Isolation Levels #

sentences.en: Mapping to Isolation Levels #
sentences.zh: 映射到隔离级别 #

blocks.html_en: Isolation levels in databases define semantics of concurrent access - what happens when two concurrent transactions access the same data. I'll cover the most common levels used in modern databases. In SQL databases you can set the isolation level explicitly per transaction. There is no similar high-level API in RocksDB, but you can build different levels yourself with lower-level APIs.

sentences.en: Isolation levels in databases define semantics of concurrent access - what happens when two concurrent transactions access the same data.
sentences.zh: 数据库中的隔离级别定义并发访问的语义 - 当两个并发事务访问相同数据时会发生什么。

sentences.en: I'll cover the most common levels used in modern databases.
sentences.zh: 我将介绍现代数据库中最常见的级别。

sentences.en: In SQL databases you can set the isolation level explicitly per transaction.
sentences.zh: 在 SQL 数据库中，您可以为每个事务显式设置隔离级别。

sentences.en: There is no similar high-level API in RocksDB, but you can build different levels yourself with lower-level APIs.
sentences.zh: RocksDB 中没有类似的高级 API，但您可以使用较低级别的 API 自行构建不同级别的 API。

blocks.html_en: Read uncommitted - the lowest isolation level, where the only guarantee is that concurrent transactions won't physically corrupt the database. This permits dirty reads - a transaction seeing uncommitted data from another transaction. Impossible in RocksDB because transaction writes sit in a buffer that gets applied to the database atomically on commit.

sentences.en: Read uncommitted - the lowest isolation level, where the only guarantee is that concurrent transactions won't physically corrupt the database.
sentences.zh: 未提交读 - 最低的隔离级别，唯一的保证是并发事务不会物理损坏数据库。

sentences.en: This permits dirty reads - a transaction seeing uncommitted data from another transaction.
sentences.zh: 这允许脏读——一个事务看到来自另一个事务的未提交的数据。

sentences.en: Impossible in RocksDB because transaction writes sit in a buffer that gets applied to the database atomically on commit.
sentences.zh: 在 RocksDB 中这是不可能的，因为事务写入位于缓冲区中，该缓冲区在提交时自动应用于数据库。

blocks.html_en: Read committed - a transaction sees only committed data, including data committed by other transactions after it started. You get this with a plain db.get, or when transactions don't use snapshots for reads. Two consecutive gets can see different values mid-transaction:

sentences.en: Read committed - a transaction sees only committed data, including data committed by other transactions after it started.
sentences.zh: 读取已提交 - 事务只能看到已提交的数据，包括其他事务在启动后提交的数据。

sentences.en: You get this with a plain db.get, or when transactions don't use snapshots for reads.
sentences.zh: 您可以通过普通的 db.get 获得此信息，或者当事务不使用快照进行读取时获得此信息。

sentences.en: Two consecutive gets can see different values mid-transaction:
sentences.zh: 两次连续的获取可以在事务中看到不同的值：

blocks.html_en: Snapshot isolation - all reads in the transaction see a consistent snapshot of the database, and the transaction succeeds only if no updates it made conflict with updates made by other transactions since the snapshot was created. Can be achieved by enabling snapshots for both reads and write conflict detection - set_snapshot covers only conflict detection, and reads have to go through the transaction's snapshot(). You avoid non-repeatable reads, but the write skew anomaly is possible.

sentences.en: Snapshot isolation - all reads in the transaction see a consistent snapshot of the database, and the transaction succeeds only if no updates it made conflict with updates made by other transactions since the snapshot was created.
sentences.zh: 快照隔离 - 事务中的所有读取都会看到数据库的一致快照，并且仅当自快照创建以来它所做的更新不与其他事务所做的更新发生冲突时，事务才会成功。

sentences.en: Can be achieved by enabling snapshots for both reads and write conflict detection - set_snapshot covers only conflict detection, and reads have to go through the transaction's snapshot().
sentences.zh: 可以通过为读取和写入冲突检测启用快照来实现 - set_snapshot 仅涵盖冲突检测，并且读取必须经过事务的 snapshot()。

sentences.en: You avoid non-repeatable reads, but the write skew anomaly is possible.
sentences.zh: 您避免了不可重复读取，但写入倾斜异常是可能的。

blocks.html_en: The snippet below illustrates the anomaly that happens when two transactions read the same data, then each updates a different key based on what it read. Alice has two bank accounts, $100 in each. The bank allows a single account to go negative, as long as the total across both accounts stays non-negative. Two concurrent transactions each withdraw $200 from a different account:

sentences.en: The snippet below illustrates the anomaly that happens when two transactions read the same data, then each updates a different key based on what it read.
sentences.zh: 下面的代码片段说明了当两个事务读取相同的数据，然后每个事务根据读取的内容更新不同的键时发生的异常情况。

sentences.en: Alice has two bank accounts, $100 in each.
sentences.zh: Alice 有两个银行账户，每个账户 100 美元。

sentences.en: The bank allows a single account to go negative, as long as the total across both accounts stays non-negative.
sentences.zh: 银行允许单个账户为负数，只要两个账户的总额保持非负数即可。

sentences.en: Two concurrent transactions each withdraw $200 from a different account:
sentences.zh: 两笔并发交易各从不同账户提取 200 美元：

blocks.html_en: Serializable - concurrent transactions run as if they were executed in serial order. You can get something close if you use get_for_update with snapshot isolation when transactions involve only point reads - you avoid dirty and non-repeatable reads and write skew. get_for_update is a special version of get used in transactions to avoid read-write conflicts e.g. when updating a key depends on the result of a read. get_for_update triggers conflict checks the same way as put and delete do. Here is the write skew example fixed by reading the balances with get_for_update, the rest of the code stays the same:

sentences.en: Serializable - concurrent transactions run as if they were executed in serial order.
sentences.zh: 可串行化 - 并发事务运行就像按串行顺序执行一样。

sentences.en: You can get something close if you use get_for_update with snapshot isolation when transactions involve only point reads - you avoid dirty and non-repeatable reads and write skew.
sentences.zh: 当事务仅涉及点读取时，如果将 get_for_update 与快照隔离一起使用，您可以得到一些接近的结果 - 您可以避免脏和不可重复的读取和写入偏差。

sentences.en: get_for_update is a special version of get used in transactions to avoid read-write conflicts e.g. when updating a key depends on the result of a read.
sentences.zh: get_for_update 是 get 的一个特殊版本，用于事务中以避免读写冲突，例如何时更新密钥取决于读取的结果。

sentences.en: get_for_update triggers conflict checks the same way as put and delete do.
sentences.zh: get_for_update 触发冲突检查的方式与 put 和 delete 相同。

sentences.en: Here is the write skew example fixed by reading the balances with get_for_update, the rest of the code stays the same:
sentences.zh: 这是通过使用 get_for_update 读取余额来修复写入偏差的示例，其余代码保持不变：

blocks.html_en: The phantom reads anomaly is possible for transactions running range scans:

sentences.en: The phantom reads anomaly is possible for transactions running range scans:
sentences.zh: 对于运行范围扫描的事务，可能会出现幻象读取异常：

blocks.html_en: T1 commits believing the total is $100, while Alice's accounts now hold $150. The new account is a phantom - get_for_update locked the keys the scan returned, but alice.savings didn't exist yet, so there was nothing to lock.

sentences.en: T1 commits believing the total is $100, while Alice's accounts now hold $150.
sentences.zh: T1 承诺相信总额为 100 美元，而 Alice 的账户现在持有 150 美元。

sentences.en: The new account is a phantom - get_for_update locked the keys the scan returned, but alice.savings didn't exist yet, so there was nothing to lock.
sentences.zh: 新帐户是一个幻像 - get_for_update 锁定了扫描返回的密钥，但 alice. savings 还不存在，因此没有什么可以锁定的。

blocks.html_en: RocksDB has a range lock manager that locks key ranges instead of individual keys to prevent phantom reads. It was contributed in 2020, but it's not documented anywhere on the RocksDB wiki and not exposed through the C or Java APIs, so the feature is usable only from C++.

sentences.en: RocksDB has a range lock manager that locks key ranges instead of individual keys to prevent phantom reads.
sentences.zh: RocksDB 有一个范围锁定管理器，可以锁定键范围而不是单个键以防止幻读。

sentences.en: It was contributed in 2020, but it's not documented anywhere on the RocksDB wiki and not exposed through the C or Java APIs, so the feature is usable only from C++.
sentences.zh: 它是在 2020 年贡献的，但 RocksDB wiki 上没有任何记录，也没有通过 C 或 Java API 公开，因此该功能只能在 C++ 中使用。
